cities.name: Port Blair
cities.overview: Port Blair is the capital and main gateway to the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, perched on the eastern coast of South Andaman. It blends colonial history with tropical beauty, serving as the launchpad for ferries to pristine beaches and coral reefs. The city is home to the iconic Cellular Jail and offers a mix of museums, markets, and coastal drives.
cities.history: Founded as a British penal colony in 1789, Port Blair gained notoriety as the site of the Cellular Jail (1906), where Indian freedom fighters were imprisoned. It was occupied by the Japanese during World War II and became part of independent India in 1947. The Light & Sound show at the Cellular Jail narrates this dramatic past.

nearest_railhead.name: None
nearest_railhead.note: Ferries from Chennai/Kolkata or road from airport

places.name: Cellular Jail National Memorial
places.description: The iconic Cellular Jail, a symbol of India's freedom struggle, once housed revolutionaries in solitary cells. The evening Light & Sound show brings its history alive with dramatic storytelling and projections.
places.tips: Attend the 6 PM Light & Sound show for the full experience. Photography is not allowed inside the cells.

places.name: Corbyn's Cove Beach
places.description: A palm-fringed golden beach just minutes from the city centre, ideal for swimming, sunset walks, and water sports. It offers the closest beach experience to Port Blair.
places.tips: Visit in the evening for stunning sunsets. Jet-skiing and banana boat rides available on weekends.

places.name: Ross Island (Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose Dweep)
places.description: A short ferry ride away, this historic island was once the British administrative headquarters. Explore ruined churches, ballrooms, and gardens reclaimed by nature.
places.tips: Ferries depart from Phoenix Bay Jetty. Carry water and wear comfortable shoes for walking the ruins.

places.name: Mount Harriet National Park
places.description: A scenic hike or drive to the highest point near Port Blair offering panoramic views of the islands, sea, and forests. Rich in birdlife and tropical flora.
places.tips: Take the 4km trek for the best experience. Early morning visits avoid heat and crowds.

places.name: Anthropological Museum
places.description: Showcasing the culture, tools, and lifestyle of the indigenous tribes of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, including the Sentinelese and Jarawa.
places.tips: A must-visit to understand the islands' tribal heritage. Guided tours available.

cities.name: Swaraj Dweep (Havelock)
cities.overview: Swaraj Dweep, formerly Havelock Island, is the most popular island in the Andamans, famous for its world-class beaches and crystal-clear waters. It serves as the perfect base for snorkeling, scuba diving, and relaxed beach holidays.
cities.history: Named after the Indian freedom struggle slogan 'Inquilab Zindabad', the island was renamed Swaraj Dweep in 2018. It was settled by Bengali refugees after Partition and has grown into a major eco-tourism destination.

places.name: Radhanagar Beach
places.description: Often voted one of Asia's best beaches, this 2km stretch of white sand and turquoise water is perfect for swimming and sunset viewing.
places.tips: Visit at sunset for magical views. No shops nearby — carry water and snacks.

places.name: Elephant Beach
places.description: A secluded beach reachable by short boat ride or trek, famous for snorkeling and coral reefs just offshore.
places.tips: Book shared boats from Govind Nagar Jetty. Snorkeling gear available for rent.

places.name: Scuba Diving & Snorkeling Sites
places.description: World-class dive sites with vibrant coral reefs, sea turtles, and tropical fish. PADI-certified operators offer courses for beginners.
places.tips: Choose certified operators only. Best visibility November–April.

cities.name: Shaheed Dweep (Neil)
cities.overview: Shaheed Dweep, formerly Neil Island, is a laid-back paradise known for its unspoiled beaches, coral reefs, and natural rock formations. It offers a quieter alternative to Swaraj Dweep with excellent snorkeling and cycling paths through villages.
cities.history: Renamed Shaheed Dweep in 2018 to honour freedom fighters, the island was settled post-Partition and remains a peaceful agricultural community growing bananas and coconuts.

nearest_railhead.name: None
nearest_railhead.note: Ferry from Port Blair or Swaraj Dweep (1–2 hrs)

places.name: Bharatpur Beach
places.description: A sheltered beach with clear waters ideal for snorkeling right from the shore. Glass-bottom boat rides offer views of underwater life.
places.tips: Best for families and beginners. Life jackets provided for snorkeling.

places.name: Kalapathar Beach
places.description: Famous for its black rocks and dramatic sunrise views. The long stretch of sand is perfect for long walks and photography.
places.tips: Arrive early for sunrise. Strong currents — swim only in designated areas.

cities.name: Baratang Island
cities.overview: Baratang is a gateway to unique natural wonders in the Middle Andamans, featuring limestone caves, mangrove creeks, and mud volcanoes. A day-trip favourite for adventure seekers.
cities.history: Historically part of the Jarawa tribal reserve, limited access opened it to tourism in the 1990s. The area showcases the islands' geological diversity.

nearest_railhead.name: None
nearest_railhead.note: Road from Port Blair (3–4 hrs) + ferry

places.name: Limestone Caves
places.description: Stunning stalactite and stalagmite formations inside ancient limestone caves, accessible by a short trek through the forest.
places.tips: Wear sturdy shoes. Guided tours mandatory. Combine with mud volcanoes.

places.name: Mud Volcanoes
places.description: Rare bubbling mud volcanoes — one of the few accessible sites in India. A short walk through mangrove forests leads to the site.
places.tips: Best visited early morning. Do not touch the mud.

cities.name: Diglipur
cities.overview: Diglipur is the northernmost major settlement in the Andamans, known for its pristine beaches, twin islands, and Saddle Peak — the highest point in the territory.
cities.history: A former penal settlement turned agricultural hub, it remains one of the least commercialised parts of the islands.

places.name: Ross & Smith Islands
places.description: Twin islands connected by a natural sandbar that appears at low tide. Crystal waters and coral reefs make it a snorkeler's dream.
places.tips: Boat rides from Diglipur Jetty. Check tide timings.

places.name: Saddle Peak
places.description: The highest peak in the Andamans at 732m, offering breathtaking views after a steep 6km trek through dense rainforest.
places.tips: Start early with a guide. Carry water and insect repellent.

cities.name: Little Andaman (Hut Bay)
cities.overview: Little Andaman is the southernmost major island, known for its untouched beaches, waterfalls, and world-class surfing spots. It offers a raw, adventurous experience.
cities.history: Largely inhabited by the Onge tribe, tourism development is limited to protect the ecosystem and indigenous communities.

places.name: Netaji Beach
places.description: A long, untouched beach famous for surfing and bodyboarding. The white sand and powerful waves attract adventure enthusiasts.
places.tips: Surfing best November–April. Hire gear from local operators.

places.name: Whisper Wave Waterfalls
places.description: A series of cascading waterfalls surrounded by dense forest, ideal for a refreshing dip after a short trek.
places.tips: Combine with a visit to the nearby elephant training camp.

cities.name: Ross Island (Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose Dweep)
cities.overview: A tiny island just off Port Blair, once the seat of British power in the Andamans. Today it's a hauntingly beautiful mix of colonial ruins overtaken by jungle.
cities.history: Headquarters of the British Chief Commissioner until 1941, it was destroyed by an earthquake in 1941 and later by the Japanese occupation.

places.name: Church & Ballroom Ruins
places.description: Eerie yet picturesque ruins of the Presbyterian Church and the Commissioner's ballroom, now overgrown with trees and vines.
places.tips: Ferry tickets include entry. Guided walks recommended.

cities.name: Mayabunder
cities.overview: Mayabunder is a quiet town in the Middle Andamans known for its mangrove forests, sea turtle nesting beaches, and proximity to tribal areas. It offers authentic, off-the-beaten-path experiences.
cities.history: A former British settlement and penal colony area, now a peaceful administrative centre with strong local communities.

places.name: Karmatang Beach
places.description: A long, secluded beach known for olive ridley turtle nesting from December to March.
places.tips: Best for turtle watching during nesting season. Respect wildlife guidelines.
